definition: Prefer telling objects what to do rather than pulling out their data and deciding externally.
ruleOfThumb: If a method keeps asking for object internals, move the decision to that object.

citation.author: Andy Hunt & Dave Thomas
citation.bookTitle: The Pragmatic Programmer: Your Journey to Mastery

questions.prompt: What is awkward about this checkout decision logic?

options.text: The service pulls customer details and makes policy decisions externally instead of asking Customer to apply its own discount rules
options.explanation: CheckoutService must know customer tier semantics and thresholds. Moving discount behavior into Customer or a policy object reduces data-peeking and coupling.

options.text: The issue is hardcoded percentage values; extracting constants would fully solve the design
options.explanation: Constants help readability, but external decision-making over internal state still remains.

options.text: getTier should return a numeric enum for faster comparisons
options.explanation: Comparison style is not the core object-design issue.

options.text: Discount logic should always live in service classes, never in entities
options.explanation: There is no absolute rule like that. The key concern here is knowledge of internals and where behavior belongs.

questions.prompt: Why does this authorization approach become fragile as account rules evolve?

options.text: Policy logic sits outside User and Document, so AccessService must know and track multiple internal rules that can change independently
options.explanation: As plan, status, or visibility semantics change, this service accumulates branching knowledge about other objects. Behavior-local methods reduce this coupling.

options.text: The main issue is that boolean variables should be inlined to reduce memory allocations
options.explanation: Inlining booleans is a micro-optimization and unrelated to architecture.

options.text: The function should return an enum with denial reasons, which removes this design issue
options.explanation: Richer return values can help UX, but they do not resolve data-asking and externalized behavior.

options.text: Access checks should use decorators, since decorators always encapsulate authorization better
options.explanation: Decorators can organize cross-cutting concerns, but they do not automatically fix misplaced decision logic.

questions.prompt: Which version has the more problematic design if account rules keep changing every quarter?

options.text: Version A — it asks for account internals to make decisions externally, so rule changes force BillingService to track Account details
options.explanation: Version A couples service logic to account state semantics. Version B keeps renewal rules with account behavior, where those details already belong.

options.text: Version B — placing renewal logic on Account violates layering and should always be moved to a service
options.explanation: Method placement depends on domain boundaries. Here, keeping state-based renewal behavior on Account improves cohesion.
